weather.tip: Overcast morning at 21.2°C with an 83% chance of rain — carry an umbrella, and build in extra commute time for waterlogging-prone stretches today.

around.headline: Fake railway agent nabbed in Bengaluru with e-tickets worth Rs 86,000
around.summary: Police caught a man running a fake railway ticket racket, selling e-tickets worth about ₹86,000 that weren't genuine bookings. Book tatkal or general tickets only through the official IRCTC app/website or an authorized counter — never from a stranger offering an instant 'confirmed' ticket for cash.

around.headline: Man assaulted twice for objecting to public smoking in Bengaluru
around.summary: A Bengaluru man was attacked twice after asking someone nearby to stop smoking in public. If you spot a public-smoking violation, report it to civic helplines rather than confronting the smoker directly.

around.headline: Hoax bomb note found on IndiGo flight bound for Ahmedabad at Bengaluru airport
around.summary: A handwritten hoax bomb threat note found in a washroom on a Bengaluru-Ahmedabad IndiGo flight triggered a security scare and delay; an FIR has been registered. If you're flying out of Bengaluru airport soon, budget extra time for possible heightened security checks.
around.source: The Times of India / The Hindu / ThePrint

around.headline: BMRCL raises Namma Metro fines up to ₹25,000 under new penalty rules
around.summary: New rules let BMRCL fine riders up to ₹25,000 for violations, including ₹2,500 specifically for playing loud music or reels on speaker inside coaches. Keep your phone on silent or use headphones on the metro to avoid an on-the-spot fine.
around.source: The Hindu / The Economic Times / The Times of India

around.headline: GBA to launch fire audits at sensitive areas in the city, violators face criminal cases
around.summary: The Greater Bengaluru Authority is starting fire-safety audits at high-risk sites, with criminal cases threatened for violators. If you run or manage a commercial building, it's worth checking fire-safety compliance now rather than waiting for an inspection notice.

around.headline: US and Iran strikes escalate, oil tankers reported on fire in the Strait of Hormuz
around.summary: The US and Iran are targeting each other's infrastructure, with oil tankers reported ablaze in the Strait of Hormuz and Indians in the Gulf already describing a resulting job crunch. A prolonged disruption there could push fuel prices higher at home and complicate travel or work plans for anyone with Gulf ties.

events.name: Happy Streets at Brookefield
events.note: The car-free public street event returns to Brookefield one last time — a good pick for a walk, cycle, or morning outing with the family.

events.name: FIFA World Cup 2026 finale watch nights
events.note: Bengaluru eateries are staying open till 3:30 AM for the World Cup final — worth checking a venue near you if you want to watch with a crowd.

events.name: Channapatna Craft Experience at NGMA Bengaluru
events.note: The National Gallery of Modern Art is hosting a hands-on showcase of Channapatna's traditional wooden toy craft — a solid outing if you want something cultural and kid-friendly.

events.name: India Watch Weekend — Bangalore Watch Days
events.note: A gathering for watch collectors and enthusiasts — worth planning ahead for if that's your hobby.

street_smart.title: A man outside the railway reservation counter says he can get you a 'confirmed' tatkal ticket right now for a fee — no app, no waiting. Do you take it?
street_smart.based_on: Real case reported today: Bengaluru police caught a fake railway agent operating with e-tickets worth roughly ₹86,000 that weren't genuine bookings (The Times of India).
street_smart.situation: You're at a busy station and need a ticket urgently. A man with no railway uniform offers to book you a 'confirmed' tatkal ticket on the spot for a service fee, showing you a phone screen with what looks like a valid PNR. What do you do?

choices.text: Pay him and take the printed or screenshotted ticket he hands you.
choices.feedback: This is exactly the racket Bengaluru police just busted: touts generate look-alike tickets, or resell someone else's genuine booking, printed or screenshotted to look valid. You only find out it's fake at the boarding gate — too late to rebook, and your money is gone.

choices.text: Decline, and book only through the official IRCTC app/website or an authorized counter, even if it means waiting.
choices.feedback: Correct. A ticket booked through IRCTC or an authorized counter is tied to your own login and ID, and you can verify it yourself immediately — you're never trusting a stranger's word for something you can check yourself in seconds.

choices.text: Ask him to show an ID or authorization badge before deciding.
choices.feedback: A badge or ID shown by a street tout can be just as fake as the ticket. Asking to see it feels like due diligence, but it doesn't actually confirm anything — only your own IRCTC login can.

choices.text: Pay him, but photograph the ticket to verify on the IRCTC app before travel.
choices.feedback: By the time you verify, you've already paid and the tout is long gone. If it turns out invalid, there's no one to get a refund from. Verify before paying, not after.

takeaways: A ticket only exists once you can see it under your own IRCTC login — anything shown to you first on someone else's phone can't be trusted.
Genuine railway staff never need to sell you a ticket for cash on the platform or outside the counter.
If you're offered an instant tatkal ticket you can't verify yourself, walk away and try the IRCTC app or the 139 railway helpline instead.

quiz.q: Bengaluru police this week caught a fake railway agent operating with e-tickets worth roughly how much?
quiz.why: Fake ticket rackets rely on victims being in a hurry — booking only through the IRCTC app or an authorized counter is the one check that reliably defeats them, since you can verify the PNR yourself instantly.

quiz.q: Under BMRCL's new Namma Metro penalty rules, what's the fine for playing loud music or reels on speaker in a coach?
quiz.why: The new rules allow fines up to ₹25,000 for more serious violations — the ₹2,500 speaker-noise fine is on the lower end of a much wider penalty scale BMRCL just introduced.

quiz.q: Oil tankers were reported on fire this week in which strait, as US and Iran strikes on infrastructure escalated?
quiz.why: Roughly a fifth of the world's seaborne oil passes through the Strait of Hormuz, which is why disruptions there tend to move global crude prices — and pump prices back home — within days.

quiz.q: Bengaluru's High Court building is popularly known by what nickname, reflecting the number of departments it originally housed?
quiz.why: 'Attara Kacheri' means 'eighteen offices' in Kannada. It's often confused with Vidhana Soudha across the road, which houses the state legislature and secretariat, not the High Court.

quiz.q: In what year was the Reserve Bank of India established?
quiz.why: The RBI began as a privately owned institution in 1935, under British rule, and was only nationalised in 1949 — two years after independence.
